content.problemStatement:
We say that a binary string (a string containing only characters '0' and '1') is pure if it does not contain either of the strings "0101" or "1010" as a subsequence.

Recall that string T is a subsequence of string S if we can delete some of the letters of S (possibly none) such that the resulting string will become T.

You are given a binary string SSS with length NNN. We want to make this string pure by deleting some (possibly zero) characters from it. What is the minimum number of characters we have to delete?
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains a single string S with length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the minimum number of characters we have to delete from S.

sampleTestCases.explanation: We can delete the first and third character of our string. There is no way to make the string pure by deleting only one character.
We can delete the first, third, and fifth character of our string. There is no way to make the string pure by deleting only one or two characters.
The given string is already pure, so the answer is zero.
The given string is already pure, so the answer is zero.
The given string is already pure, so the answer is zero.

solution.approach: We can solve this problem by iterating over the string S and maintaining a counter for the number of characters we have to delete. We can use a state machine to track the current state of the string. If the current character is '0' and the previous character is '1', we increment the counter. If the current character is '1' and the previous character is '0', we increment the counter. If the current character is the same as the previous character, we do not increment the counter.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) with a single pass over the string S.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) as we only use a constant amount of space.
